title: Basic Julia
text: A Pluto.jl notebook v0.19.38 frontmatter chapter 1 section 1 order 1 title "Basic Julia" layout "layout.jlhtml" tags "julia basics" description "" using Markdown using InteractiveUtils using PlutoUI md""" `basic julia.jl` | Version 1.2 | last updated Feb 3 2022 """ md" A first glance at the Julia language This notebook briefly summarizes some of the basic Julia syntax that we will need for the problem sets. " Markdown.MD Markdown.Admonition "warning", "This notebook is taken from", md""" Computational Thinking , a live online Julia Pluto textbook. computationalthinking.mit.edu https computationalthinking.mit.edu , original notebook https github.com mitmath 18S191 blob Fall20 lecture notebooks Basic%20Julia%20syntax.jl """ md" Variables We can define a variable using ` ` assignment . Then we can use its value in other expressions " x 3 y 2x md"By default Julia displays the output of the last operation. You can suppress the output by adding ` ` a semicolon at the end. " md"We can ask what type a variable has using `typeof` " typeof y md" Functions" md"We can use a short form, one line function definition for simple functions " f x 2 x md"Typing the function's name gives information about the function. To call it we must use parentheses " f f 10 md"For longer functions we use the following syntax with the `function` keyword and `end` " function g x, y z x y return z^2 end g 1, 2 md" For loops" md"Use `for` to loop through a pre determined set of values " let s 0 for i in 1 10 s s i end s end md"Here, `1 10` is a range representing the numbers from 1 to 10 " typeof 1 10 md"Above we used a `let` block to define a new local variable `s`. But blocks of code like this are usually better inside functions, so that they can be reused. For example, we could rewrite the above as follows " function mysum n s 0 for i in 1 n s s 1 end return s end mysum 100 md" Conditionals `if`" md"We can evaluate whether a condition is true or not by simply writing the condition " a 3 a 5 md"We see that conditions have a Boolean `true` or `false` value. We can then use `if` to control what we do based on that value " if a 5 "small" else "big" end md"""Note that the `if` also returns the last value that was evaluated, in this case the string `"small"` or `"big"`, Since Pluto is reactive, changing the definition of `a` above will automatically cause this to be reevaluated """ md" Arrays" md" 1D arrays `Vector`s " md"We can make a `Vector` 1 dimensional, or 1D array using square brackets " v 1, 2, 3 typeof v md"The `1` in the type shows that this is a 1D array. We access elements also using square brackets " v 2 v 2 10 md"Note that Pluto does not automatically update cells when you modify elements of an array, but the value does change." md"A nice way to create `Vector`s following a certain pattern is to use an array comprehension " v2 i^2 for i in 1 10 md" 2D arrays matrices " md"We can make small matrices 2D arrays with square brackets too " M 1 2 3 4 typeof M md"The `2` in the type confirms that this is a 2D array." md"This won't work for larger matrices, though. For that we can use e.g." zeros 5, 5 md"Note that `zeros` gives `Float64`s by default. We can also specify a type for the elements " zeros Int, 4, 5 md"We can then fill in the values we want by manipulating the elements, e.g. with a `for` loop." md"A nice alternative syntax to create matrices following a certain pattern is an array comprehension with a double `for` loop " i j for i in 1 5, j in 1 6 md""" Appendix """ TableOfContents

title: More Julia
text: A Pluto.jl notebook v0.19.46 frontmatter chapter 1 section 2 order 1 title "More Julia" layout "layout.jlhtml" tags "julia basics" description "" using Markdown using InteractiveUtils using DataFrames using DataFrameMacros using Chain chain using PlutoUI md""" `more julia.jl` | Version 1.1 | last updated May 10 2022 | created by Daniel Schmidt https github.com danieljschmidt """ md""" More Julia """ md""" The purpose of this notebook is to make you familiar with Julia syntax that is frequently used in the course material. It also points out the peculiarities of Pluto notebooks. """ md""" Named tuples and keyword arguments Named tuples Like many other programming languages, Julia allows you to create tuples. The elements of tuples can be accessed by using the corresponding index """ standard tuple 1, 2 standard tuple 1 md""" Julia also has named tuples. Just like standard tuples, named tuples allow you to access an element using its index, but alternatively you can also access an element by its name. """ named tuple a 1, b 2 named tuple.a md""" This is convenient because it means that you do not need to remember if some parameter is the first element in a tuple or the second one. Below you can see an alternative way of creating named tuples that is frequently used in the course material """ let a 1 named tuple2 a, b 2 a is equivalent to a a end md""" Keyword arguments A similar syntax with a semicolon is used for keyword arguments that are identified by name and not by their position as normal function arguments """ function some function a, b 1 return a b end some function a 3, b 5 let a 3 some function a, b 5 a is equivalent to a a end md""" Vectorization with dot syntax You can apply a function to all elements of a vector by using the dot syntax """ 1,2,3 .^ 2 log. 1,2,3 md""" Note how Julia usually does things in a way that's mathematically consistent. Look at the following code. """ A ones 3, 3 exp A exp. A A^2 A .^ 2 md""" What would Python, R or Matlab do? """ md""" The pipe operator The pipe operator | makes it possible to write down nested function calls in a more readable way. For example, the two expressions below do the same thing """ round log named tuple.b named tuple.b | log | round md""" In case you use R The | operator in Julia is similar to the % % operator in R. """ md""" Unicode characters You can use Greek letters and other Unicode characters in your Julia code. For example, type "\alpha" in the cell below without the quotation marks and press Tab on your keyboard. This should create an \alpha symbol. """ md""" See the Julia documention https docs.julialang.org en v1 manual unicode input for a list of supported Unicode characters. For Greek letters, the abbreviations are the same as in LaTeX. """ md""" The \in symbol An elegant way of writing loops is to use the \in symbol instead of writing "in". The \in symbol can be created by typing "\in" and pressing Tab. """ i^2 for i ∈ 1 5 md""" Multiple dispatch Multiple dispatch means, that you can define specific methods or any combination of input types . """ struct Dog name end ╠═╡ struct Cat name owner end ╠═╡ makes this sound x Dog "Bark bark, I am x.name " makes this sound x Cat "Meeoow, I am x.name , and I love x.owner " makes this sound x "bla bla" ╠═╡ constantin Cat "Constantin", "Franz Joseph" ╠═╡ makes this sound 1 ╠═╡ makes this sound constantin ╠═╡ bello Dog "Bello" makes this sound bello md""" Working with data Here are a few simple examples of working with DataFrames in Julia. If you are familiar with pandas, dplyr or Stata, have a look at this cheatsheet https dataframes.juliadata.org stable man comparisons . Also, have a look at the documentation of DataFrameMacros.jl https jkrumbiegel.github.io DataFrameMacros.jl stable which makes working with DataFrames much more convenient. """ md""" Creating a `DataFrame` """ df1 DataFrame x 1 10 md""" Transforming mutating a column """ transform df1, x ByRow x sqrt x transform df1, sqrt x uses transform from DataFrameMacros.jl md""" The ``` chain``` macro """ md""" The ``` chain``` macro works similar to the pipe operator. In the code for this course, the ``` chain``` macro is often applied to data frames together with macros from the ```DataFrameMacros``` package. """ df2 DataFrame A 1,2,2,1 , B randn 4 md""" Consider the data frame above. Let's say you would like to add up the values in the B column separately for each value of A take the absolute value of the resulting sums of B values. Using the ``` chain``` macro, we can perform this task with relatively concise code """ chain df2 begin groupby A combine sum B , automatically named C sum B specify name transform abs C end md""" Without the ``` chain``` macro, the code would look like this """ begin df groups groupby df2, A df sum combine df groups, sum B , C sum B transform df sum, abs C end md""" Pluto notebooks General advice Press F1 to see shortcuts for Pluto notebooks. Ctrl Click on an underlined variable or function to jump to its definition. Use the Live docs in the bottom right corner to get more information about any Julia function or object. Check the Github wiki https github.com fonsp Pluto.jl wiki for more information on Pluto notebooks. Automated updating of cells When changing a function or variable, Pluto automatically updates all affected cells. For example, change the value of d to some other number and see how the following cell updates automatically """ d 5 d 10 md""" This is different from jupyter notebooks in which you have to update related cells manually. The automated updating the advantage that you do not have to keep in mind the order in which to evaluate cells. However, it can also be annoying if some of the affected cells take a long time to run. At the time of writing, there is no way to turn off the automated updating but you can always manually disable cells with long run times by clicking on the three dots in the top right corner of a cell. """ md""" Only one expression per cell Pluto notebooks only allow one expression per cell. If you nevertheless want to place several expressions into the same cell, you have to use a begin ... end block """ begin e 10 f e 5 end md""" Cannot reuse variable names """ md""" Use `let` blocks to specify variable names locally. """ let g 3 end g md""" Deactivate cells """ ╠═╡ sleep 5 ╠═╡ md""" Imported Packages """ TableOfContents g 1 g 2

title: Assignment 3: Dynamic programming
text: A Pluto.jl notebook v0.20.24 frontmatter chapter 2 section 4 order 4 title "Assignment 3 Dynamic programming" layout "layout.jlhtml" tags "lifecycle" description "" using Markdown using InteractiveUtils This Pluto notebook uses bind for interactivity. When running this notebook outside of Pluto, the following 'mock version' of bind gives bound variables a default value instead of an error . macro bind def, element format off return quote local iv try Base.loaded modules Base.PkgId Base.UUID "6e696c72 6542 2067 7265 42206c756150" , "AbstractPlutoDingetjes" .Bonds.initial value catch b missing end local el esc element global esc def Core.applicable Base.get, el ? Base.get el iv el el end format on end using BenchmarkTools belapsed using DimensionalData using CairoMakie, AlgebraOfGraphics using DataFrames, DataFrameMacros, Chain using Interpolations using MarkdownLiteral markdown using PlutoUI Slider, Select, CheckBox using PlutoUI TableOfContents using PlutoTest test md""" `dynamic programming.jl` | Version 1.1 | last updated March 19 2026 """ md""" Assignment 3 Dynamic programming """ md""" Part 1 Solving the sequence problem Let's start by solving the simple ``J`` period household's problem from the lecture using the standard sequence formulation . ```math \begin align &\max c j, a j 1 j 0 ^ J 1 \sum j 0 ^ J 1 \beta^j u c j \\ &\begin aligned \text subject to & c j a j 1 w \cdot y 1 r a j \quad \text for all j\\ & a 0 \text given \\ & a J \geq 0. \end aligned \end align ``` """ md""" Task 1 ``J`` periods by hand In this task, you use solve the ``J`` period model analytically . 👉 Use the formulas from the lecture to solve the optimal choices of a household that lives for ``J`` periods for given prices and the parameters given above CRRA flow utility . """ function solve household analytical γ, β, J , r, w , y, a₀ inc w y note how to use `sum ` like a ∑ operator 𝒴 sum 1 1 r ^j inc for j ∈ 0 J 1 1 r a₀ compute optimal consumption in the initial period c₀ 𝒴 sum 1.0 FIX for j ∈ 0 J 1 Create `DimVector`s to save consumption and saving This container type allows custom indexing, e.g. j ∈ 0, ..., J 1 j dim Dim j 0 J 1 c zeros j dim, name consumption a next zeros j dim, name savings c j At 0 c₀ a next j At 0 y w 1 r a₀ c₀ for j ∈ 1 J 1 last period's choice aⱼ₊₁ is current period's state aⱼ aⱼ a next j At j 1 Compute optimal choices in period j cⱼ 1.0 FIX aⱼ₊₁ 0.0 FIX Save optimal choices to containers a next j At j aⱼ₊₁ c j At j cⱼ end DimStack a next, c end md""" ``\Delta r`` bind Δr fixed Slider 0.05 0.01 0.05, default 0.01, show value true """ answer1 md""" You answer goes here ... """ md""" Appendix to Task 1 """ par γ 2.0, β 0.9, J 10 prices slider r 1 par.β 1 Δr fixed, w 1.0 L"β 1 r \approx % round par.β 1 prices slider.r , sigdigits 4 " prices test r 1 par.β 1 0.09, w 1.0 income y 1.0, a₀ 0.0 function u c, γ if c ≤ 0 return Inf elseif γ 1 return log c else return c^ 1 γ 1 γ end end analytical solution test solve household analytical par, prices test, income test analytical solution test.consumption j At 0 ≈ 0.8825961987723083 test analytical solution test.consumption j At 5 ≈ 1.0723246453729767 test analytical solution test.savings j At 5 ≈ 0.4460335406985083 analytical solution solve household analytical par, prices slider, income chain analytical solution begin DataFrame stack Not j data mapping j L"age j ", value "", layout variable visual ScatterLines draw facet linkyaxes false , figure size 500, 200 end md""" Part 2 Dynamic programming You are now going to replicate the manual solution from Task 1 using dynamic programming. You only work with the backward solution , comparing both Value Function Iteration Task 3 and the Endogenous Grid Method Task 4 . You can find the forward solution in the Appendix to Part 2 . """ md""" Solving backward """ function fill last period container, a grid, par, prices, income value, consumption, savings container y income β, J par r, w prices inc y w COMMENT savings j At J 1 . 0.0 COMMENT consumption j At J 1 . inc . a grid FIX COMMENT value j At J 1 . u. consumption j At J 1 , Ref par return nothing end """ Fill the value and policy functions for ``j ∈ 0, ..., J 2`` into a container. `container` needs to contain the values for the last period ``j J 1`` `container` is a `DimStack` that is indexed by `a` and `j` """ function solve backwards VFI container, a grid, par, prices, income consumption, savings, value container β, J par y income r, w prices inc y w For levels of current wealth aⱼ a and intended savings aⱼ₊₁ a next how much can the household consume Hint Use the budget constraint for age j c a, a next 0.0 FIX for j ∈ J 2 1 0 for a ∈ a grid a next a grid try all possible choices for next period cs c. a, a grid R u. cs, Ref par . value j At j 1 FIX find the best one v, a i opt findmax R a next opt a grid a i opt save the best one value a At a , j At j v savings a At a , j At j a next opt consumption a At a , j At j c a, a next opt end end end """ Fill the value and policy functions for ``j ∈ 0, ..., J 2`` into a container. `container` needs to contain the values for the last period ``j J 1`` `container` is a `DimStack` that is indexed by `a` and `j` """ function solve backwards EGM container, a grid, par, prices, income consumption, savings, value container β, J, γ par y income r, w prices inc y w Euler equation get cⱼ₋₁ cⱼ cⱼ 1 r ^0 FIX Budget constraint get aⱼ₋₁ aⱼ, cⱼ₋₁ cⱼ₋₁ aⱼ 0 inc FIX for j ∈ J 1 1 1 aⱼ endo a grid cⱼ consumption a At a grid , j At j COMMENT cⱼ₋₁ endo get cⱼ₋₁. cⱼ aⱼ₋₁ get aⱼ₋₁. aⱼ endo, cⱼ₋₁ endo cⱼ₋₁ itp linear interpolation aⱼ₋₁, cⱼ₋₁ endo, extrapolation bc Line aⱼ itp linear interpolation aⱼ₋₁, aⱼ endo, extrapolation bc Line COMMENT cⱼ₋₁ exo cⱼ₋₁ itp. a grid aⱼ exo aⱼ itp. a grid savings a At a grid , j At j 1 . aⱼ exo consumption a At a grid , j At j 1 . cⱼ₋₁ exo end end md""" Appendix to Part 2 """ par test γ 2.0, β 0.9, J 15 par sol test aₘᵢₙ 0.1, aₘₐₓ 1.1, N EGM 10, N VFI 200 sol analytical test solve household analytical par test, prices test, income md""" Part 3 Comparison """ md""" `N VFI` bind N VFI accuracy Slider 10 10 200, default 10, show value true `N EGM` bind N EGM accuracy Slider 10 10 100, default 10, show value true `aₘᵢₙ` bind aₘᵢₙ accuracy Slider 5 0.25 0, default 0.25, show value true `aₘₐₓ` bind aₘₐₓ accuracy Slider 0 0.25 5, default 0.25, show value true Plot type bind visual slider Select Lines, Scatter, ScatterLines , default ScatterLines """ answer5 md""" You answer goes here ... """ md""" Appendix to Task 5 """ par accuracy γ 2.0, β 0.9, J 15 par EGM accuracy aₘᵢₙ aₘᵢₙ accuracy, aₘₐₓ aₘₐₓ accuracy, N EGM N EGM accuracy par VFI accuracy aₘᵢₙ aₘᵢₙ accuracy, aₘₐₓ aₘₐₓ accuracy, N VFI N VFI accuracy sol analytical accuracy solve household analytical par accuracy, prices test, income md""" Task 6 Speed 2 points Let us measure the time it takes to solve the model using VFI and EGM. 👉 First, un check the checkbox below. Skip benchmarks bind skip benchmarks CheckBox default true Once you do that the benchmarks are run. This can take a while whenever you change a slider. """ md""" `N VFI` bind N VFI speed Slider 10 10 200, default 10, show value true `N EGM` bind N EGM speed Slider 10 10 100, default 10, show value true """ answer6 md""" You answer goes here ... """ md""" Appendix to Task 6 """ par speed γ 2.0, β 0.9, J 60 par EGM speed aₘᵢₙ 2.0, aₘₐₓ 0.25, N EGM N EGM speed par VFI speed aₘᵢₙ 2.0, aₘₐₓ 0.25, N VFI N VFI speed md""" Appendix to Part 2 """ function make grid min, max, length sort unique 0.0 range min, max length end function create container a grid, J Create `DimArrays`s to save consumption and saving for each age j and each possible asset level a This container type allows custom indexing, e.g. j At 5 , a At 0.0 for getting the value consumption savings at age 5 and asset level 0.0 a dim Dim a a grid j dim Dim j 0 J 1 value zeros a dim, j dim , name value policies consumption zeros a dim, j dim , name consumption savings zeros a dim, j dim , name savings return DimStack value, consumption, savings end md""" Solving forward """ function solve forward container backward, a grid, par, income J par a₀ a grid findfirst ≥ income.a₀ , a grid put initial assets on grid savings, consumption container backward ADD COMMENT j dim Dim j 0 par.J 1 a path zeros j dim, name savings c path zeros j dim, name consumption for j ∈ 0 J 1 if j 0, set a a₀, otherwise ... CONTINUE COMMENT aⱼ₋₁ j 0 ? a₀ a path j At j 1 ADD COMMENT cⱼ itp linear interpolation a grid, consumption j At j , a At a grid extrapolation bc Line aⱼ itp linear interpolation a grid, savings j At j , a At a grid , extrapolation bc Line c path j At j cⱼ itp aⱼ₋₁ a path j At j aⱼ itp aⱼ₋₁ end return DimStack c path, a path end function solve household par, par sol, prices, income method VFI make sure that a valid `method` is provided if method ∉ VFI, EGM throw ArgumentError "There is no method `method`. `method` must be either VFI or EGM." end if method VFI length par sol.N VFI else length par sol.N EGM end a grid make grid par sol.aₘᵢₙ, par sol.aₘₐₓ, length container bw create container a grid, par 1. Solve backward a. Solve last period fill last period container bw, a grid, par, prices, income b. Solve all other periods if method VFI solve backwards VFI container bw, a grid, par, prices, income else method EGM solve backwards EGM container bw, a grid, par, prices, income end 2. Solve forward sol fw solve forward container bw, a grid, par, income container bw, sol fw end sol EGM test solve household par test, par sol test, prices test, income method EGM let df vcat DataFrame sol EGM test.sol fw , DataFrame sol analytical test , source method "EGM", "analytical" chain df begin stack Not method, j data mapping j, value, layout variable, color method, linestyle method visual visual slider draw facet linkyaxes false , figure size 700, 200 end end sol VFI test solve household par test, par sol test, prices test, income method VFI let df vcat DataFrame sol VFI test.sol fw , DataFrame solution EGM.sol fw , DataFrame sol analytical test , source method "VFI", "analytical" chain df begin stack Not method, j data mapping j, value, layout variable, color method, linestyle method visual visual slider draw facet linkyaxes false , figure size 700, 200 end end sol EGM accuracy solve household par accuracy, par EGM accuracy, prices test, income method EGM sol VFI accuracy solve household par accuracy, par VFI accuracy, prices test, income method VFI let df vcat DataFrame sol VFI accuracy.sol fw , DataFrame sol EGM accuracy.sol fw , DataFrame sol analytical accuracy , source method "VFI", "EGM", "Analytical" chain df begin stack Not method, j data mapping j, value, layout variable, color method, linestyle method visual visual slider draw facet linkyaxes false , figure size 700, 200 end end time egm skip benchmarks || belapsed solve household par speed, par EGM speed, prices test, income method EGM time vfi skip benchmarks || belapsed solve household par speed, par VFI speed, prices test, income method VFI Δpc time egm time vfi 1 skip benchmarks ? md"No benchmarks to display." md""" With N VFI speed gridpoints, VFI takes time vfi seconds. With N EGM speed gridpoints, EGM takes time egm seconds. Δpc 0 ? md"EGM is round 100 abs Δpc , digits 1 % faster" "EGM is round 100 abs Δpc , digits 1 % slower" """ md""" Appendix """ INSTRUCTIONS fun name, pre "" markdown """ pre Explore the function ` fun name ` and try to rougly understand what it does. Then replace ` FIX ` with appropriate code replace ` COMMENT ` and with a brief description of what the subsequent line of code does """ md""" Task 2 The last period 1 point INSTRUCTIONS "fill last period ", "👉 Let us start in the last period ``j J 1``." """ md""" Task 3 Value function iteration 2 points INSTRUCTIONS "solve backwards VFI ", "👉 Let us consider the remaining periods ``j ∈ 0, ..., J 2``." """ md""" Task 4 Endogenous grid method 2 points INSTRUCTIONS "solve backwards EGM ", "👉 Let us consider the remaining periods ``j ∈ 0, ..., J 2``." """ TableOfContents md""" Assignment infrastructure """ function wordcount text stripped text strip replace string text , r"\s" " " words split stripped text, ' ', ' ', '.', ',', ' ', ' ', '"', ' ', ' ', '\'' length filter "" , words end test wordcount " Hello, it's me. " 4 test wordcount "This doesn't really matter." 5 show words answer md" approximately wordcount answer words " function show words limit answer, limit count wordcount answer if count 1.02 limit return show words answer else return almost md"You are at count words. Please shorten your text a bit, to get below limit words ." end end limit1 20 show words limit answer1, limit1 md""" 👉 How does the solution change when you change ``\Delta r``. Explain. limit1 words """ limit5 50 show words limit answer5, limit5 md""" Task 5 Accuracy In Part 1 we solved for the analytical solution of this model, so let us compare the solutions of the numerical solutions to the analytical solution. 👉 Compare the numerical solutions VFI and EGM to the analytical solution. Play around with the sliders below and investigate how the accuracy of the numerical solution changes. Document some of your findings. limit5 words """ limit6 50 show words limit answer6, limit6 md""" 👉 Play around with the number of grid points for EGM and VFI and document what you find about the speed of the two methods. Given your findings in Task 5, is it fair to compare methods at the same number of grid points? limit6 words """ cell id " " string PlutoRunner.currently running cell id grpmid cell id group members firstname "Ella Louise", lastname "Flores" , firstname "Padraig", lastname "Cope" , firstname "Christy", lastname "Denton" grpnid cell id group number 99 if group number 99 || group members 1 .firstname "Ella Louise" && group members 1 .lastname "Flores" markdown """ danger "Note " Before you submit , please replace the randomly generated names in this cell grpmid by the names of your group and put the right group number in this cell grpnid . """ end markdown """ Before you submit ... 👉 Make sure you have inserted your names in this cell grpmid and put the right group number in this cell grpnid . 👉 Make sure that that all group members proofread your submission. 👉 Go to the very top of the notebook and click on the symbol in the very top right corner. Export a static html file of this notebook for submission. The source code is embedded in the html file. """ function format members group members names map group members do firstname, lastname firstname " " lastname end join names, ", ", " & " end md""" submitted by format members group members group group number """ format members group members

text: A Pluto.jl notebook v0.20.21 frontmatter chapter 2 section 2 order 2 title "Assignment 1 Household balance sheets" layout "layout.jlhtml" tags "lifecycle" description "" using Markdown using InteractiveUtils This Pluto notebook uses bind for interactivity. When running this notebook outside of Pluto, the following 'mock version' of bind gives bound variables a default value instead of an error . macro bind def, element format off return quote local iv try Base.loaded modules Base.PkgId Base.UUID "6e696c72 6542 2067 7265 42206c756150" , "AbstractPlutoDingetjes" .Bonds.initial value catch b missing end local el esc element global esc def Core.applicable Base.get, el ? Base.get el iv el el end format on end using PlutoUI using Chain, DataFrames, DataFrameMacros using AlgebraOfGraphics, CairoMakie using MarkdownLiteral markdown begin using CategoricalArrays using StatsBase formatter from, to, i kwargs... i function CategoricalArrays.cut x AbstractArray, w AbstractWeights, ngroups Integer labels formatter cut x, StatsBase.quantile x, w, 1 ngroups 1 ngroups extend true, labels end function CategoricalArrays.cut x AbstractArray, w AbstractWeights, ps Vector labels formatter, extend true cut x, StatsBase.quantile x, w, ps extend, labels end formatter2 from, to, i kwargs... "bottom 50", "top 50" i formatter3 from, to, i kwargs... "bottom 50", "middle 40", "top 10" i formattern from, to, i kwargs... "Q i" cut2 x, w cut x, weights w , 0.5 extend true, labels formatter2 cut3 x, w cut x, weights w , 0.5, 0.9 extend true, labels formatter3 cutn x, w, n cut x, weights w , n labels formattern end using PlutoTest test using HypertextLiteral md""" `lifecycle data.jl` | Version 1.2 | last updated March 10 2026 """ md""" Assignment 1 Household Balance Sheets For this assignment you will use the Survey of Consumer Finances SCF . You can use `get scf year ` e.g. `get scf 2022 ` to download the Summary Extract Public Data . For example """ md""" Task 1 Networth across ages 3 points The figure below shows household wealth across age groups. """ md""" use consistent y axes bind same scale CheckBox default false """ answer1 1 md""" You answer goes here ... """ answer1 2 md""" You answer goes here ... """ md""" Task 2 Assets 4 point Have a look at the SCF Networth Flow Chart https www.federalreserve.gov econres files Networth%20Flowchart.pdf which explains how networth is composed in the SCF. Can you guess what asset categories are important for the bottom 50, middle 40 and top 10? 👉 2.1 | 2 points Adjust the variables below. Fill `nfin vars` with non financial asset classes, and `fin vars` with financial asset classes of your choice. The figure will automatically change to show your chosen variables. Use consistent y axes bind same scale2 CheckBox default false """ nfin vars BUS fin vars LIQ answer2 2 md""" You answer goes here ... """ md""" Task 3 Aggregation 3 points The dataframe `data df₀` collects shows partially aggregated data from the SCF and the CEX Survey of Consumer Expenditures . """ md""" 👉 3.1 | 1 point Rename the columns so that they match the variable names of simple overlapping generations model from the lecture. The cell below has it wrong, please fix """ answer3 2 md""" You answer goes here ... """ md""" Appendix to Task 3 """ md""" End of Assignment """ md""" Some more stuff dis organized """ md""" Incomes Consumption """ cex categories "expense erikcat1" "ex clothing jewelry", "expense erikcat10" "ex o transportation", "expense erikcat11" "ex health educ", "expense erikcat12" "ex o non durables", "expense erikcat13" "ex furniture", "expense erikcat14" "ex entertainment durables", "expense erikcat15" "ex vehicles", "expense erikcat2" "ex housing", "expense erikcat3" "ex food home", "expense erikcat4" "ex food away", "expense erikcat5" "ex alcohol tobacco", "expense erikcat6" "ex personal care", "expense erikcat7" "ex communication media", "expense erikcat8" "ex entertainment", "expense erikcat9" "ex utilities" md""" The distributions of income and net worth """ md""" Appendix """ TableOfContents my sum x... length x 0 ? sum x... 0.0 md""" DataDeps SCF, CEX, MacroHistory """ import CSV scf checksums Dict 1989 "3600d39fa908f2b6b32a63518fe38d2b7ca7d9c8047e947b054ea2e7d36f7b9e", 1992 "d86da7dc07819adb1f08683d7b04f782d2fb1c135ea94880ce4b6550db3c0ccd", 1995 "c28169ca73855a1b1f22a999655ea573fc5391b11884b9272c9cf2bf1ee5c442", 1998 "69fda43abc88df203f03b9a9e8cf5bf5bc0a626da4b99d44f2fd11dee2c0b11e", 2001 "28f5548f91d5f851ad643d9c172e00ecfeb1d6fe47126fb2496af5b980f75ffd", 2004 "bb08a6122a25348f6507fcd9377511820972108e8642d6ec1ab3ddf262c21071", 2007 "85324789b2ab6f5e5dfc05a8de294e4fd837f7e2174f41e5be5e146435a68aa9", 2010 "a85ee57748ec28b3366a4f0b9446ec8b0c34710f14097b6a6e03089c9ad8823a", 2013 "f13ed12756798c7e696dbf56ec26438cc2e0c46de4c3343afd3fd05ddfb9e6e8", 2016 "11e92c267f333fe10678c9cbb9752c10290085c35abd7c52f7f21c8df45dc468", 2019 "87766da9024f7b6742d277c955234cf6bee439248fbc5f689c81d91880fd1b05", import StatFiles const SCF YEARS 1989 3 2022 begin using DataDeps ENV "DATADEPS ALWAYS ACCEPT" true using DataDeps datadep str for year in SCF YEARS register DataDep "SCF year ", "", "https www.federalreserve.gov econres files scfp year excel.zip", scf checksums year checksum Union String,Vector String ... , Optional, if not provided will generate post fetch method unpack end register DataDep "MacroHistory", "", "http data.macrohistory.net JST JSTdatasetR5.dta" , Base.get el iv el el end format on end using Optim using CairoMakie, AlgebraOfGraphics using Distributions using Expectations Expectations, expectation using DataFrames using Chain, DataFrameMacros using LaTeXStrings, Latexify using PlutoUI md""" `simple income risk.jl` | Version 0.1 | last updated June 6 2025 """ md""" Uninsurable Income risk """ md""" Specifying income distributions """ dists DiscreteNonParametric 8.0 , 1.0 , DiscreteNonParametric 9.0, 6.5 , 0.6, 0.4 , DiscreteNonParametric 10.0, 5.0 , 0.6, 0.4 , DiscreteNonParametric 11.0, 3.5 , 0.6, 0.4 , DiscreteNonParametric 12.0, 2.0 , 0.6, 0.4 list of dists dist "var var dist " for dist in dists md""" Using the package `Expectations.jl` ... to compute expectations of functions of `y 1`. """ md""" Household's problem """ function U c₀, par details false y₀, y₁ dist, u, β, r par if u isa Pair u label last u u first u else u label "" end a₁ y₀ c₀ c₁ y₁ 1 r a₁ y₁ 𝔼u₁ expectation y₁ u c₁ y₁ , y₁ dist 𝔼U u c₀ β 𝔼u₁ if details return 𝔼u₁, 𝔼U, a₁, c₀, 𝔼y₁ mean par.y₁ dist , Var y₁ var par.y₁ dist , u label else return 𝔼U end end md""" Pick a distribution, bind y₁ dist Select list of dists , and check its mean, variance and a few random draws below. """ mean y₁ dist var y₁ dist rand y₁ dist, 10 expectation x x, y₁ dist expectation x x^2, y₁ dist expectation x x, y₁ dist ^2 utilities log "log", c c^ 1 1 2 1 1 2 "CRRA 1 2 ", c 15 c 1.0 2 c^2 "quadratic" md""" Pick a flow utility function bind u Select utilities """ md""" Compare solution for different distributions """ utilities c max y₁ dist, r, y₀ y₀ minimum y₁ dist 1 r eps function solve household y₁ dist, u u c 15 c 1.0 2 c^2 u log β 0.95 par y₀ 8.0, y₁ dist, β, r 1 β 1, u sol optimize c₀ U c₀, par , eps , c max par info par.y₀, 𝔼y₁ mean par.y₁ dist , Vary₁ var par.y₁ dist U sol.minimizer, par details true end solve household y₁ dist, u solve household dists 1 , u solve household dists 2 , u solve household dists 3 , u df solve household. permutedims dists , utilities | vec | DataFrame let chain df begin groupby u label transform Δ𝔼U bycol 𝔼U first 𝔼U stack a₁, Δ𝔼U, c₀ , Var y₁, u label transform variable latexify variable data mapping Var y₁ L"income risk \mathbb V y 1 ", value "", row variable, color u label "flow utility" visual ScatterLines draw facet linkyaxes false , figure size 400, 350 end end data df mapping Var y₁, 𝔼U, color u label "flow utility" visual ScatterLines | draw md""" Appendix """ TableOfContents

""" md""" Task 4 An economy with multiple types of households 3 points So far, our economy consisted of one type of household. At each point in time two cohorts of this type of household are alive because this household lives for two periods . Let us now assume that there are three types of households `` P, M, R `` with incomes ``y P y M y R``. You can think of poor , middle class and rich . text: A Pluto.jl notebook v0.20.4 frontmatter chapter 2 section 1 order 1 title "Baseline two period model" layout "layout.jlhtml" tags "lifecycle" description "" using Markdown using InteractiveUtils This Pluto notebook uses bind for interactivity. When running this notebook outside of Pluto, the following 'mock version' of bind gives bound variables a default value instead of an error . macro bind def, element format off quote local iv try Base.loaded modules Base.PkgId Base.UUID "6e696c72 6542 2067 7265 42206c756150" , "AbstractPlutoDingetjes" .Bonds.initial value catch b missing end local el esc element global esc def Core.applicable Base.get, el ? Base.get el iv el el end format on end using Contour Contour using Optimization using OptimizationOptimJL using DataFrames, DataFrameMacros, Chain using PlutoUI using PlutoUI Slider using CairoMakie md""" `two periods.jl` | Version 1.0 | last updated March 10 2024 """ md""" Two baseline two period model """ md""" Recall the consumer's choice problem with two goods from your first microeconomics course. The household solves \max c 0, c 1 0 u c 0, c 1 subject to the constraint c 0 \frac p 1 p 0 c 1 \leq \frac m p 0 . Good ``0`` is the numeraire. We consider the following utility function """ u c, γ γ 1 ? log c c^ 1 γ 1 γ begin u₁ x₁, x₂, γ, β u x₁, γ β u x₂, γ end md""" bind u 1 Select u₁ """ md""" 1. Utility function and indifference curves Let's visualize indifference curves using a contour plot. First, pick parameters ``\beta, \gamma`` """ par₀ β 0.95, γ 2.0 md""" Then, we chose grids on the domain of u . Motivation Inequality and the Mortage Boom See the notebook `macroeconomic trends.jl`. Others Shape Our Economic Decisions Our economic decisions are influenced by our peers. Our consumption of visible goods depends on the consumption of our peers' visible goods. Neighbors of lottery winners buy bigger cars https www.aeaweb.org articles?id 10.1257 aer.101.5.2226 Nonrich consume more visible goods when top incomes rise https www.mitpressjournals.org doi abs 10.1162 REST a 00613 Home owners are less happy with their house when a big house is built nearby https papers.ssrn.com sol3 papers.cfm?abstract id 3378131 """ md""" Model We consider a lifecycle model where consumers live for two periods. ```math \begin align &\max c 1, c 2, h u c 1, s \beta u c 2, s \\ &\begin aligned \text s.t. &u c, s c^ 1 \xi s^\xi \\ &s s h, \bar h h \phi \bar h \\ &c 1 p h y d \\ &c 2 d 1 r y \end aligned \end align ``` """ Base. kwdef struct ComparisonsOLGModel β 0.95 discount factor ξ 0.3 utility weight of consumption ϕ 0.7 strength of the comparison motive G 0 0 0.5 0 0 0.5 0 0 0 p 1.0 groups "bottom 50", "middle 40", "top 10" group weights weights 0.5, 0.4, 0.1 end par0 ComparisonsOLGModel rate β 1 1 r md""" Simulating the model """ par md md""" ``\beta`` discount factor assume ``\beta 1 1 r `` ``\theta`` utility weight of housing ``\phi`` strength of the comparison motive ``p`` house price """ function solution β, ξ, ϕ, p, G, groups , y₀ κ₀ ξ 1 ξ 1 β κ₁ 1 β ξ p κ₂ 1 ξ h next y, h κ₁ y κ₂ ϕ G h function m next y, h h n h next y, h β . y p κ₀ h n ϕ G h end h₀ I ϕ κ₂ G \ κ₁ y₀ h next, m next, h₀ end begin y₀ 0.5, 1, 2 T 10 end weight df par DataFrame t 1 y panel 2, t y panel 2, t 1 y panel 3, t 1.1 y panel 3, t 1 end m next, h next, h₀ solution h sim , 1 h₀ m sim , 1 . m next y₀, h₀ for t in 2 T h sim , t h next y panel , t , h sim , t 1 m sim , t m next y panel , t , h sim , t 1 end sim df grp par.groups i , t for i in 1 N, t in 1 T | vec | DataFrame sim df.y y panel | vec sim df.h h sim | vec sim df.m m sim | vec chain sim df begin transform house2income h y, mort2income m y leftjoin weight df par , on grp disallowmissing end end sim df0 simulate par0, y₀, solution par0, y₀ , T md""" ``\beta`` bind β1 Slider 0.7 0.05 1.0, show value true, default 0.95 discount factor ``\xi`` bind ξ1 Slider 0.1 0.1 0.9, show value true, default 0.3 utility weight of housing ``\phi`` bind ϕ1 Slider 0.1 0.1 0.9, show value true, default 0.7 strength of bind gRP1 Slider 0.0 0.1 1.0, show value true, default 0.0 bind gRM1 Slider 0.0 0.1 1.0, show value true, default 0.0 bind gRR1 Slider 0.0 0.1 1.0, show value true, default 0.0 """ G1 gPP1 gPM1 gPR1 gMP1 gMM1 gMR1 gRP1 gRM1 gRR1 par1 ComparisonsOLGModel β β1, ξ ξ1, ϕ ϕ1, G G1 sim df vcat sim df0, simulate par1, y₀, solution par1, y₀ , T , source parameters baseline, sliders md""" Appendix """
